rate_limiter: Per-tenant token buckets with a global coordination layer. Each gateway node holds local buckets refilled at the tenant's contracted rate, while a lightweight gossip or Redis-backed shared counter reconciles aggregate consumption across nodes every few hundred milliseconds. Fairness comes from per-tenant configured rates; the distributed reconciliation prevents a tenant from exceeding their quota by spreading traffic across nodes.

Deficit Round Robin (DRR) scheduling at the gateway. Instead of rate-limiting per tenant independently, treat all tenants as queues served by a fair scheduler with per-tenant deficit counters proportional to weight. This guarantees max-min fairness of throughput: idle tenants donate capacity to busy ones automatically, and no tenant can starve another. It shifts the problem from 'admit/reject' to 'order of service' under a shared capacity budget.

Stochastic sampling with per-tenant probabilistic rejection (sketch-based). Maintain Count-Min sketches of recent request rates per tenant on each node and reject requests with probability rising as a tenant's estimated share exceeds its fair quantile. This is memory-cheap, needs no exact cross-node state, and degrades gracefully under cardinality explosion, trading exactness for O(1) space and approximate fairness.

Auction/credit-market allocation. Give each tenant a periodic credit budget; requests 'bid' credits, and the gateway clears capacity to the highest aggregate value while enforcing that no tenant's clearing price starves it below a floor. Spare capacity is auctioned, letting tenants who value bursts pay (in credits) for them. Fairness is economic: equal endowments yield equal long-run service, but priorities become expressible.

Control-theoretic adaptive limiting. Model each tenant's allowed rate as the output of a PID or AIMD controller whose setpoint is target latency or queue depth, not a fixed RPS. When the shared backend saturates, all tenants' limits contract proportionally (multiplicative decrease); as headroom returns they grow additively. Fairness emerges from symmetric controller gains, and the system self-tunes to actual backend capacity rather than a guessed static number.

Hierarchical virtual-time scheduling (WFQ / Start-time Fair Queuing). Assign every request a virtual finish time based on tenant weight and request cost, and serve in virtual-time order. Nesting this hierarchically (org -> team -> key) gives fairness at multiple granularities simultaneously, so a noisy key cannot consume its whole org's share. It cleanly handles heterogeneous request costs by weighting virtual time by estimated work, not request count.
cache: Per-tenant partitioned cache with dynamic resizing. Statically slice the cache into per-tenant segments, then let a feedback loop reallocate capacity based on each tenant's marginal hit-rate gain (miss-ratio curves). A burst from one tenant can only evict its own entries, guaranteeing isolation, while the resizer reclaims under-utilized segments. This converts the noisy-neighbor problem into a constrained optimization of total hit rate subject to per-tenant floors.

Cost-aware admission control instead of eviction control (TinyLFU-style). Gate what enters the cache using a frequency sketch, and weight admission by a per-tenant fairness factor so a bursty tenant's flood of one-hit-wonders is rejected at the door rather than thrashing the working sets of others. Admission filtering is cheaper than reactive eviction and naturally suppresses scan-heavy bursty patterns.

Fair eviction via weighted LRU clocks. Run a single shared cache but make the eviction policy tenant-aware: each entry's eviction priority is scaled by how far its tenant is over its fair share of occupancy. Over-quota tenants' entries are evicted first; under-quota tenants are protected. This keeps the simplicity of one pool with one global hot set, while embedding max-min fairness directly into the victim-selection rule.

Two-tier cache with per-tenant burst absorbers. A small fast L1 per tenant absorbs bursts locally without polluting the shared L2; only entries proven hot in L1 are promoted to the shared tier. Bursts are quarantined in bounded per-tenant L1s, so transient spikes never reach or evict shared content. The promotion gate acts as a frequency filter that protects the common pool's stability.

Latency-SLO-driven shared budget with control loop. Don't fix capacity per tenant; instead measure each tenant's observed hit latency and have a controller throttle cache writes (or shrink share) for tenants whose bursts push others' p99 above SLO. Fairness is defined on the outcome (latency) rather than the resource (bytes), so the system tolerates bursts that don't actually harm anyone and clamps only the ones that do.

Predictive/segmented caching by access pattern. Classify tenants online into stable vs bursty/scan-like workloads (e.g., via reuse-distance estimation) and route them to policies tuned for each: LFU-style retention for stable, bypass-or-FIFO for scan-heavy. Bursty scanners get a small protective ring buffer that cannot evict the long-lived working sets of stable tenants. Fairness comes from matching policy to behavior rather than treating all access streams identically.
convergence: Prove convergence to second-order stationary points via strict-saddle avoidance with explicit rates. Assume the landscape satisfies a strict-saddle property (every saddle has a direction of negative curvature bounded away from zero) and show perturbed gradient descent escapes all saddles in polynomially many steps, converging to an approximate local minimum. The new contribution is sharpening the iteration-complexity dependence on the curvature gap and dimension under a relaxed smoothness assumption.

Use the Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz (KL) inequality framework to get convergence of the whole iterate sequence (not just function values). For functions satisfying a KL property with exponent theta, prove the iterates form a Cauchy sequence and converge to a single critical point with rate determined by theta. A new theorem would establish the KL exponent for a structured non-convex class (e.g., low-rank or neural objectives) and translate it into a finite-length trajectory bound.

Trajectory-based / dynamical-systems analysis via the gradient-flow ODE and its discretization gap. Study the continuous flow's convergence using a Lyapunov function, then bound the discretization error to transfer the result to discrete steps. The novelty is a Lyapunov function (perhaps a Bregman or entropy-regularized potential) tailored to a non-convex geometry that certifies escape from poor regions and convergence to a target manifold of minimizers.

Stochastic/SDE limit and exit-time analysis for SGD. Model SGD as a discretized SDE and prove convergence in distribution to a neighborhood of minima, characterizing the metastable exit times from saddle/local-min basins via large-deviations theory. A fresh theorem could quantify how noise scale and batch size govern the rate of escaping shallow basins, giving a convergence guarantee to flat minima that depends on the geometry of the loss.

Gradient-dominance / Polyak-Lojasiewicz (PL) condition with relaxation. Many practical non-convex problems satisfy a local PL inequality even without convexity; under PL, gradient descent converges linearly to the global minimum value. A new result would prove a *local* or *averaged* PL condition holds for a broad non-convex family (e.g., over-parameterized models) and derive the corresponding global linear-rate theorem, possibly with an explicit basin-of-attraction radius.

Geometric/manifold approach via overparameterization and the Neural Tangent regime. Show that in a suitable scaling limit the non-convex objective becomes effectively convex along the optimization trajectory (constant tangent kernel), yielding global convergence. The theorem would relax the infinite-width assumption: prove convergence holds once width exceeds an explicit polynomial threshold, with a rate degrading gracefully as the kernel drifts, bridging finite networks to the idealized limit.
matchmaking: Bayesian skill rating with uncertainty-aware pairing (TrueSkill-style). Model each player's skill as a distribution, not a point, and match to minimize predicted match-quality variance (expected draw probability). New players get wide priors that converge fast, so they aren't unfairly thrown against settled high-rated players. Fairness means each match is a near-coin-flip in expectation, and rating updates are confidence-weighted so a single lucky game doesn't distort placement.

Multi-objective optimization over a candidate pool. Treat matchmaking as solving for the assignment that jointly optimizes skill balance, ping/latency, wait time, and role/party composition with explicit weights and fairness constraints (e.g., team skill-sum gap below a cap). Use a periodic batch solver (bipartite matching or ILP) over the waiting pool rather than greedy one-at-a-time pairing, which yields globally fairer lobbies at the cost of slightly higher latency.

Role- and contribution-aware rating instead of a single MMR. Decompose skill into per-role and per-mechanic dimensions and balance teams so each role matchup is fair, not just the aggregate team strength. This prevents stacked-lane or stacked-role blowouts that a single scalar rating hides. Fairness is defined at the position level, producing matches that feel balanced moment-to-moment rather than only on paper totals.

Outcome-fairness via dynamic handicapping and win-probability calibration. Continuously predict each match's win probability and adjust matchmaking (or in-game spawn/economy parameters) to drive predicted outcomes toward 50/50, then audit realized win rates per cohort to detect systematic unfairness. The goal is calibrated competitiveness: not equal ratings but equal *expected experience*, with feedback loops correcting any group (new players, off-peak regions) that consistently gets unfair matches.

Behavioral and experience-quality matchmaking, not just skill. Incorporate signals like toxicity reports, quit/AFK rate, and queue-dodging into matching so well-behaved players are grouped together and disruptive ones are isolated or penalized. Fairness here targets the *quality* of the session, recognizing that a skill-balanced match ruined by a griefer is not fair; this complements MMR with a parallel sportsmanship/reliability score.

Market/queue-time auction balancing fairness against availability. Frame matchmaking as a constrained search where match quality is traded against wait time per player, allowing players (or the system) to relax skill tolerance as they wait. Use a widening-bracket mechanism with explicit fairness floors so low-population regions or times still get bounded-unfairness matches rather than infinite queues. This treats wait time as a first-class fairness dimension, since being unable to play at all is the worst unfairness.
